topic.keyword: depleted dad syndrome symptoms and solutions
topic.angle: Being an involved father shouldn't mean losing yourself—but for too many men, it does.

hook: How to Stop Depleted Dad Syndrome: 6 Steps

slides.title: Step 1: Name it out loud
slides.body: Say "I'm burnt out as a dad" to one person. Not admitting weakness—admitting reality.

slides.title: Step 2: Reclaim 15 minutes daily
slides.body: Before you walk in the door. Sit in the car. No phone, no podcast. Just breathe.

slides.title: Step 3: Stop performing fatherhood
slides.body: Your kids need a present dad, not a perfect one. Drop one thing you're forcing.

slides.title: Step 4: Ask for one specific thing
slides.body: Not "I need help." Try: "Can you take mornings Saturday so I can sleep in?"

slides.title: Step 5: Talk to someone outside
slides.body: Partner can't fix this alone. Friend, voice AI, therapist—someone who isn't counting on you tonight.

cta.title: Save this. Send to a friend who needs it.
cta.body: 24/7 voice-first mental health support — link in bio. 🔗

caption_body:
You're doing everything right on paper—showing up, providing, being involved. So why does it feel like you're disappearing?

Depleted Dad Syndrome isn't about loving your kids less. It's about losing yourself in the role until there's nothing left for you. No hobbies. No energy. No version of you that isn't "Dad."

This isn't sustainable. And it's not selfish to admit that.

You don't need permission to take care of yourself—but if you're waiting for it, here it is.

→ Link in bio for MindCore AI. 7-day trial €1.99.
